system_prompt:
You are extracting load-bearing passages from one chapter of a guitar method book.

The user will give you the verbatim text of one chapter. Extract every passage
that does any of the following:

- teaches a technique (how to play, fingering, hand position, picking pattern)
- defines a concept (a chord type, a scale, a position system, a terminology)
- gives a rule (when to do X, what to avoid, what to prefer)
- shows a load-bearing example (an exercise that illustrates a method's
  characteristic move)

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  { "quotes": [
      { "topic": "<short topic label>",
        "verbatim_quote": "<exact substring of the input>",
        "why_load_bearing": "<one sentence on why this passage matters>",
        "page": <int, optional>
      },
      ...
  ] }

CRITICAL RULES:
1. `verbatim_quote` MUST be an EXACT substring of the input text. Do not
   paraphrase. Do not add words. Do not fix typos. Copy character-for-character.
2. If you can't find a substantial passage in the input that fits a topic,
   skip the topic — don't fabricate.
3. Skip prose that is purely biographical, motivational, or transitional.
   Keep prose that teaches.
4. Aim for 3-15 quotes per chapter. A chapter with only one load-bearing
   passage gets one quote; a dense chapter gets many.
5. `topic` is a short label (2-6 words). Use the book's own terminology
   when possible.
6. `why_load_bearing` is one sentence in YOUR voice explaining the
   significance — not a paraphrase of the quote.
user_prompt:
Chapter 7: Module 5 - More Advanced Arranging Techniques
Pages 213-263 of the book.

<<<CHAPTER TEXT

Module 5 Introduction

Welcome to Module 5 – the final module of the course.

In this module, we are going to give you some more advanced ideas to bring your chord melody
arrangements to the next level.

Throughout this module, you’ll discover substitutions – a world of harmonic color to give your
arrangements a unique personality.




 A substitution simply means replacing one chord with another.




We’ll also look at how to spontaneously add licks and chord phrases to your arrangements to
provide more interest and excitement.

Finally, we’ll tie everything together by giving you some handy tips on how to get your arrangement
performance-ready.

Let’s dive in!


Substitution Approach #1 - Tritone Substitution

If you have been studying jazz guitar for any length of time, you are sure to have come across the
concept of tritone substitution.

This harmonic technique is perhaps the most useful harmonic substitution in jazz. It’s one of the
defining sounds of the genre – applying this type of substitution instantly gives that classic jazz
sound.

If you’re just going to experiment with one substitution technique, pick this one.




What is Tritone Substitution?




 Tritone substitution is the process of substituting one chord for another a tritone away.




A tritone may sound cryptic, but it is what it sounds like: “tri – tone” meaning 3 tones away:




To find a tritone, start with a note then move upwards 3 tones (which equals 3 whole steps or 6
frets).


Interestingly:

Look what happens if you descend from a C note down 3 tones:




We end up on the same note name – whether we go up or down from C, we reach an F#.

This is why a tritone interval is sometimes referred to as a split octave – as the tritone interval splits
an octave precisely in half:




Let’s now have a look at how we can use this interval as a substitution technique.


Compare these two shell voicings with the roots a tritone away – C7 and F#7:




   Play audio example 5-1




Notice how although the root has changed, the other notes are exactly the same:




C7 has E as the 3rd and Bb as the 7th.

F#7 has E as the 7th and A# (which is the same as Bb) as the 3rd.

The guide tones have essentially swapped places between these two chords.




 Because the two chords have those 3rd and 7th as common tones, they can be substituted for
 one another.


Tritone Substitution Examples

To demonstrate tritone substitution in action, let’s take this turnaround at the end of the A section
of Stompin’ At The Savoy:




   Play audio example 5-2




You can substitute the 2nd chord, the Bb7 with a dominant chord a tritone away, i.e. E7:




   Play audio example 5-3


You can also substitute the final chord, Ab7 with D7:




   Play audio example 5-4




Note that this also works for other types of chords beside dominants.

The 3rd chord is an Ebm7, a minor chord. You can use a dominant chord a tritone away from the root,
A7:




   Play audio example 5-5




This is both substituting the chord and changing its quality.


I can take this one step further and experiment with different chord qualities on the substitutions.


 Although this isn’t a tritone substitution in the strictest sense (the term usually applied to
 dominant chords), you can often create great effects by experimenting with major and minor
 chords as well.




An example:

Looking at the 2nd chord we have substituted in this progression, an E7. Let’s see what happens if we
make that chord an Emaj7 instead:




   Play audio example 5-6


Let’s try making ALL the substitute chords maj7 now:




   Play audio example 5-7




Keep in mind that these substitution techniques are essentially rewriting the harmony for a jazz
standard. This will cause issues if you are playing with others who are assuming the chord progression
is still the standard one for a song.

But:

When it comes to solo guitar, you have the freedom to do these kinds of substitutions liberally as it’s
just you on stage.




 Tritone Substitution Rule - You can replace any chord with another whose root is a tritone
 away from the root of the original chord.


Harmonizing a Melody with Tritone Substitution

Here’s an example of how you can use tritone substitution underneath a melody.

A good place to start is to spot where a dominant chord is in the progression as they are often good
candidates.

Looking at our arrangement of Fly Me To The Moon, we can see a dominant chord in the first phrase
here:




Replacing the G7 chord with its tritone substitute (Db7) we get this:




   Play audio example 5-8


Another option is to play the original chord on beat 1, then add the tritone substitution later in the bar
on other melody notes (this often makes more sense to the ears):




   Play audio example 5-9




 Task: experiment with tritone substitutions in your arrangement and explore the vast tonal
 possibilities that lie within this technique.




A word Of caution: with this and the other substitution techniques I’m outlining in this chapter, it’s
easy to go overboard with this kind of thing and get too tricky, which is the equivalent of adding “too
much spice to your curry”.

Keeping things simple most of the time and just adding a substitution occasionally to brighten up the
arrangement is the approach I find works best – a good arrangement is all about subtlety!


Substitution Approach #2 - Major Chords to Minor Chords

In this section, you will learn 2 substitutions for major chords that you will often find used in jazz
charts.




Major to Minor Substitution 1 - Relative Substitution

Notice how certain major and minor keys share the same key signature:




   Play audio example 5-10




The reason is that they share the same notes in both scales.

In this case, the G major and E natural minor scales have identical notes, it’s just that G major starts
on a G and E minor starts on an E.




 G major and E minor are called relative key signatures to each other because they share the
 same key signature.


If these two keys have the same notes, you can often substitute the chords from those keys for each
other. We call this a relative substitution.

A Gmaj7 chord is the same notes as Em9 without the root:




An Em7 chord is an inversion of a G6 chord:




Em7 is chord vi in the key of G major, and Gmaj7 is chord III in the key of E minor.




 Rule: you can replace the I chord of a key with the vi chord of the key.


Major to Minor Substitution 1 - Chord I to Chord iii

There is another major to minor substitution that’s common in jazz.

As we covered above, Em7 could be considered an inversion of Gm6.

We can observe a similar phenomenon when we compare the iii chord of the key of G major (Bm7) to
its I chord (Gmaj7):




Bm7 is an inversion of Gmaj9.

These kinds of substitutions are great to use as it gets you away from being stuck with the root tone
in the bass the whole time, which can get a bit stale after a while.




 Rule: you can substitute a major chord for chord iii of its key.


Examples of Major to Minor Substitutions

Here’s an example of these substitutions in action.

Let’s begin with a phrase from the jazz standard Out Of Nowhere:




   Play audio example 5-11




That Gmaj7 chord that lasts for 2 bars sounds a bit static when we play this as a solo guitar
arrangement.


So in the second bar, let’s apply the iii chord substitution – reharmonizing that bar to Bm7 instead:




   Play audio example 5-12


In a similar way, you can apply a relative substitution to the 2nd bar of the next line, reharmonizing
with Em7:




   Play audio example 5-13




 This process is referred to as chord addition – adding extra chords to the harmony.




This is a useful technique for solo jazz guitar as it provides yet another point of interest and helps
create more energy in the performance. Keeping things fresh and moving is your job as a solo jazz
guitarist.



 Task: Experiment with these major to minor substitutions in your arrangements – explore adding
 extra chords to the chord progression with these substitutions to provide more interest.


Substitution Approach #3 - Minor 7th for Dominant 7th



 Pat Martino was a great proponent of what’s known as the "minor conversion concept" - a theory
 that any chord you see in a progression could be substituted for a minor chord instead.




For improvisation, this makes things very efficient – if you know your minor scales well and have
mastered this concept, you can solo over any harmonic situation.

We can take this idea and adapt it to chord melody arranging.

In fact:

We’ve already dipped our toes into this concept in the last lesson, where you discovered how to
substitute major chords with minor chords.

Now in this lesson, I’ll show you a technique to use minor chords in the place of dominant
chords.


Minor to Dominant Substitution Explained

Take a look at the following ii V I progression in the key of C:




   Play audio example 5-14




Let’s look carefully at the ii and the V chord – the ii chord is actually quite similar to the V.

In fact:

They are nearly the same chord in terms of the notes they use – the only difference being the C of
the Dm7 moving to the B of the G7.

Joe Pass would often mention that the ii and the V chord can be interchanged with one another, and
this is the reason – they are very similar in their construction.

So:

This means they can be substituted for one another.




 Minor for Dominant Substitution Rule

 You can use a minor 7 chord from the 5th of any dominant chord (which is like using the iim7 that
 belongs to the dominant chord).

 For example: an F7 chord’s 5th interval is C. So you could play Cm7 over F7 to get an interesting
 sound.


Examples of Minor for Dominant Substitution

This substitution is a handy way of embellishing the harmony to create more interest over long
dominant chords.

By moving chords between ii and V instead of sticking just to the V chord, you can help the music
sound less stagnant.

Let’s take this blues in Bb as an example:

As you can hear, this makes the music sound more energetic with the addition of the minor chords in
front of the dominants.



 Task: experiment with this dominant to minor substitution in your arrangements, in particular
 oscillating between the ii chord and the V chord.


Substitution Approach #4 - Back Cycling

“Back cycling” is another common form of substitution that solo jazz guitarists use to create more
interest in a chord progression.

You will also see this harmonic device used liberally in common jazz standards.

The term “back cycling” refers to the circle of 5ths:




When one cycles anti-clockwise (backward) through the cycle of 5ths, we get a sequence of root
tones that are separated by the interval of a 4th.

This cycle of root notes a 4th apart is the most common root movement in Western music.

In fact, a ii V I progression is an example of this “cycle 4 root movement”:


How to Use Backcycling as a Substitution

Because each chord in the back cycle of 4ths leads naturally to the next, we can use this theory to our
advantage in our arrangements.

Let’s take one of my original compositions, Number 8 with pretty stock standard harmonisations:

As you can hear, this creates a great deal more interest in this chord progression, yet sounds very
natural to the ears.

This technique is an excellent way to bring more energy to the music and have the arrangement still
sound very ‘inside’.

Note that this technique (like the other substitutions we have covered) won’t necessarily work well if
you are playing in a band as the changes may clash with the original chord chart the other players are
working off. But it’s a very effective reharmonization technique for solo jazz guitar arrangements.




 Back cycling substitution rule - you can add chords to the chord progression of a song based
 on the ‘back cycle’, i.e. the circle of fourths.




 Task: Experiment with this substitution in your arrangements and observe the effect this has on
 the sound of the harmony.


Substitution Approach #5 - Common Tone Substitution

If you’re keen on a more modern jazz sound to the likes of Jonathan Kreisberg or Kurt Rosenwinkel,
then this substitution approach is for you.

This substitution allows you to substitute any chord for another chord, as long as the new chord has a
note in common with it that you use as the melody note on top.

This creative approach can generate very unusual and provocative sounds in your arrangements.

Here’s how it works:

Let’s take the classic jazz standard All The Things You Are as an example:




   Play audio example 5-19


The Eb7 chord in the 3rd bar has a G as the melody note on top:




I can substitute a G7 chord instead, with G as the highest note, and adapting the fingering of the
passage to suit:




   Play audio example 5-20


Because that melody note repeats, I could even add another common tone substitution in the same
bar, a Db9#11:




   Play audio example 5-21




You could really go to town on this technique. Here’s an example of even more melodic common tone
substitutions in this passage:




   Play audio example 5-22




Although these chords make for a very complex progression, because the melody is familiar to the
ears it still makes sense to the listener.

For that reason, it may be desirable to add these kinds of substitutions a bit later in the song form


when a recap of a melodic motive happens, so that the audience can still recognize the melody
despite the unorthodox harmony.




 Melodic common tone substitution rule: substitute any chord for another chord, as long as
 the new chord has a note in common with it that you use as the melody note on top.




 Task: experiment with this colorful substitution method in your arrangements – there is a
 dynamic universe of sound within this simple technique.



The substitutions we have covered in the previous lessons will open your arranging up to an ocean of
possibilities – explore each one in turn as you move forward with your arrangements.

In the next lesson, you’ll look at alternative ways of spicing up a chord melody performance through
the use of jazz licks and chord phrases…


Adding Jazz Licks and Prases

Here’s another technique for spicing up your arrangements of chord melodies – but it’s one of the
most important ones.

Here we get to experiment with swapping out the original melody with our own single lines and
improvised chord phrases.

It may seem counterintuitive to abandon the melody in your arrangement (it is called ‘chord melody’
after all).

But:

Especially when it comes to solo jazz guitar, things can get repetitive quickly, and ways to create
more variety are an essential part of your toolbox.




 Joe Pass would often not play the complete melody in an arrangement and place his own phrases
 in place to keep the audience on its toes.




Whenever you get a jazz chart with a melodic figure that repeats (e.g. a repeat of an A section) – this
can be a good place to use this technique.

Let’s have a look at it in action.


Chord Phrase Example

Here’s our arrangement of Fly Me To The Moon that we completed earlier in this course.

Starting at bar 17, we have a repeat of the opening melodic motive and chords:




   Play audio example 5-23




Instead of playing this melody verbatim again, you can mix things up by throwing in a classic Joe
Pass style chord phrase instead:




   Play audio example 5-24




This could be ‘pre-meditated’ or more spontaneous if you have some chord phrases ready to go.

Notice how this creates more tension and excitement in the music instead of playing the standard
melody again.


Single-Note Lick Example

You don’t need to only play chord phrases, you could use single-note lines for this technique too.

Looking at bar 21 of our Fly Me To The Moon arrangement:




   Play audio example 5-25




You can see there is a minor ii V i progression at that point (Bm7b5 – E7(b9) – Am7), so you can use
a minor ii V i jazz lick in the place of the melody at this point.


Joe Pass would play these spontaneous phrases with a high degree of rubato – pushing and pulling
the tempo for dramatic effect:




   Play audio example 5-26




Adding chord phrases and licks is the key to injecting more spontaneity and pizzazz into your
arrangements.

One of the most difficult things in jazz guitar is coming up with a chord melody arrangement on the
spot, so most guitarists need to pre-arrange a chord melody before a performance.

But:

By swapping in your own lines and chord phrases here and there in your arrangement when you
perform, you can generate a lot more excitement and energy for the audience.



 Task: experiment with adding jazz licks or chord phrases you know in place of the melody in your
 chord melody arrangement.


Completing Your Arrangement and Getting Ready to Perform

Now that you have all the tools in your toolkit to be a proficient chord melody arranger, there are just
a few final steps we need to cover to ensure success on the concert stage.




 Arranging a chord melody is only 50% of the work – the other 50% is turning your arrangement
 into a great performance on your guitar.




Here are some tips to get your performance of your chord melody arrangement in tip-top shape.




Use a Metronome

Using a metronome, especially for solo jazz guitar, is crucial to developing a strong performance.

Because solo jazz guitar is unaccompanied, it’s very easy to let the rhythm slide more and more
behind the beat.

Lagging too far behind the beat gives the whole arrangement a ‘sleepy’ feel which can result in a
lackluster performance.

A little flexibility is fine here and there, but overall the rhythm needs to be alive and steady.

Practice your arrangement with a metronome as follows:

   Have the metronome click on every beat.
   Then try the metronome clicking on beats 1 and 3 only.
   Then have a go with the metronome on beats 2 and 4 only.

The final version is most desirable for jazz as it helps give the music more of a swing.


Make Time to Practice

Simple but often overlooked:

If you don’t apply yourself daily to learning an arrangement until it’s mastered, you’ll find your
progress to be frustratingly slow.

Make sure if you are committing to perform your chord melody, you apply yourself each day, even for
a short duration of 10-15 minutes – it is the proven path for getting results on your instrument.




Set Small Goals for Each Session

Don’t try to get the whole arrangement perfect in a single practice session.

Instead, methodically work through just 1 or 2 bars per session. Spend time slowly practicing the
fingering and carefully checking the notes are accurate and that everything is moving comfortably
under your fingers.

When 1-2 bars are flowing and feel good under the hands, move to the next 2 bars in your next
session and so on, gradually learning the arrangement one step at a time.

Don’t be in a rush to get the whole arrangement correct right away – that will come with daily
consistent application.


Complete Performance Example - Fle Me to The Moon

Using many of the substitution techniques we have covered in this module, check out the following
arrangement of Fly Me To The Moon as a solo guitar arrangement.

Notice how I’ve often substituted chords for other alternatives. I’ve also incorporated chord phrases
and a single line run towards the end of the arrangement for dramatic effect. An intro has also been
added as a way to set up the introspective mood of this rendition.



 Task: learn the following solo guitar arrangement of Fly Me To The Moon, or at least listen
 through and study the score to notice the various arranging techniques used.
